channel.title: Thirteen-Eighty
channel.description: Stories for the 3am listener. Short fiction written for audio, set in South Florida.
channel.itunes:author: Otto von Wachter

item.title: Channel Nine
item.description: We bought the walkie-talkies at a garage sale on Mellen Lane the first Saturday of summer vacation, nineteen ninety-three. Two dollars for the pair. Danny talked the man down from three by pointing out that one antenna had teeth marks in it, and the man looked at Danny for a s...

item.title: Dance Lessons
item.description: The counselor's word was "activity." We had done eight Thursdays on Dr. Lefkowitz's loveseat, ninety dollars a session after insurance, me on one arm of the couch and Carol on the other with her purse between us, and at the end of the eighth the doctor said we might try an act...

item.title: Kitchen Hands
item.description: They installed it over a weekend, which tells you something. Anything corporate is proud of, they roll out while you watch. The new fryers in two thousand twenty-two came with a regional manager and a sheet cake. The arm came in while the store was closed for two days of quote...

item.title: La Promesa
item.description: We buried my mother on a Saturday morning at Our Lady of Mercy, and by two in the afternoon the house in Westchester was full of people eating her food without her. Somebody's pasticho came out of the freezer, and the living room filled with men watching baseball with the soun...

item.title: Round Seven
item.description: I placed ninth in the Palm Beach County spelling bee in nineteen ninety-one. People are surprised I remember the year. Then I tell them I also remember the word, boutonniere, and that I left out the second n. I have not needed to spell boutonniere once in thirty-five years. I ...

item.title: The Claw
item.description: I do inventory for a living, which is a way of saying I notice what's supposed to be there. The Publix on Jog Road in Greenacres has four amusement machines on the vendor contract: two gumball towers, a sticker machine, and a rocket ride that hasn't rocketed since two thousand...

item.title: The Healing Line
item.description: Every Tuesday at seven in the evening, a line forms on West Forty-Ninth Street in Hialeah, outside a storefront church wedged between a cell phone repair shop and a bakery that closes at six. The church is called Iglesia El Buen Pastor, which is a lot of name for a room with f...

item.title: The Hurricane Box
item.description: My mother kept a plastic bin on the top shelf of her bedroom closet, and every year on the first of June she took it down and packed it the same way. The deed to the house. The insurance policies in a freezer bag. Her passport, and my grandmother's rings taped inside a travel ...

item.title: The Last Human Lane
item.description: I worked lane one at the Lantana toll plaza, milepost eighty-eight on Florida's Turnpike, for twenty-nine years. For the last six of those it was the only cash lane left between Miami and Fort Pierce. Everything else went SunPass. The transponder lanes don't have a gate or a l...

item.title: The Mango Tree
item.description: There is a mango tree on Spruce Street, in the Northwood neighborhood of West Palm Beach, that grows out of two properties at once. The trunk sits on the line. Not near the line. On it. I have paid for two surveys that say so, and Ernesto Quintana paid for one of his own in be...

item.title: The Request Line
item.description: I work overnights at WLKW, thirteen-eighty on the AM dial, out of a cinder-block building behind the Chevron on Lake Worth Road. One in the morning to five. The station mostly runs itself. There's a rack of computers down the hall that picks the songs, schedules the ads, and r...

item.title: The Yard Sale
item.description: I have worked yard sales on the Treasure Coast for thirty years, both sides of the table, and I can usually tell you what's wrong with one from the curb. Overpriced Pyrex. A reseller seeding the dollar box. The sale across the street took me three Saturdays, and I still got it...

item.title: Third Wednesday
item.description: I am fifty-six years old, and a Broward County judge sent me to anger management because I put a paver stone through the windshield of my own truck. I did it in a customer's driveway in Coral Springs, in front of her and her two kids, in the middle of an invoice dispute I was ...

item.title: Un-Condemned
item.description: My signature is on two hundred and eleven demolition orders. I counted them in January, in the records room on Blue Heron Boulevard, because the clerk still lets me behind the counter, and because I wanted the number exact before I decided what to do about it.
